1:
"And it came to pass after these things, that one told Joseph, Behold, thy father is sick: and he took with him his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim." The witnesses note Joseph bringing his sons to the sick Jacob. On "one told Joseph, Behold, thy father is sick," the grammatical voices note the summons. Gill and Henry note that "one told Joseph, Behold, thy father is sick" — Joseph was informed of Jacob's illness (the aged patriarch near death, 47:28-31); the summons to the dying father; the occasion of the blessing (vv.5-20); the setting of Jacob's deathbed.

On "he took with him his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the sons. Calvin and Gill note that Joseph took his two sons (Manasseh and Ephraim, born in Egypt, 41:50-52) to his father; the desire that they be blessed by the dying patriarch; the bringing of the sons for the blessing (and the adoption, v.5).

On the visit, the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the faith: Joseph's bringing his sons to be blessed — the faith that valued the patriarch's blessing (the covenant blessing) above all the honors of Egypt; the desire that his sons share the covenant (not the Egyptian inheritance only); the setting of the great deathbed scene (Jacob's faith, Hebrews 11:21). The verse displays Joseph bringing his sons to the sick Jacob.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the summons — "one told Joseph, Behold, thy father is sick," the dying father (47:28, Gill, Henry); "he took with him his two sons, Manasseh and Ephraim," the sons (41:50, Calvin, Gill); the faith (Hebrews 11:21, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the visiting Joseph. The verse marks the father: sick. The verse displays the sons: brought. The verse displays the blessing: sought. The verse displays the faith: valuing it. The visit displays Joseph bringing his sons for the blessing. The verse displays the Joseph: coming. The verse displays the sons: with him. The verse displays the deathbed: set. Joseph bringing his sons to the sick Jacob displays the faith that valued the covenant blessing: Joseph took Manasseh and Ephraim to his dying father (41:50; Hebrews 11:21) — that they might share the covenant blessing above all Egypt's honors. The verse displays the strengthened Jacob (v.2).
2:
"And one told Jacob, and said, Behold, thy son Joseph cometh unto thee: and Israel strengthened himself, and sat upon the bed." The very brief verse notes Jacob rousing himself for Joseph's visit. The grammatical voices note the rousing. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "one told Jacob... Behold, thy son Joseph cometh unto thee: and Israel strengthened himself, and sat upon the bed" — Jacob, told of Joseph's coming, gathered his failing strength and sat up on the bed; the aged patriarch rousing himself for the solemn act (the blessing); the effort of the dying man; the dignity of the occasion.

On "Israel strengthened himself," the reformed and witnesses note the effort. Gill notes that Jacob "strengthened himself" (gathered his strength) — the dying patriarch making the effort to sit up; the resolve for the blessing; the failing body roused for the solemn act.

On "sat upon the bed," the grammatical-historical note the dignity: "sat upon the bed" — Jacob sitting up (the posture of the blessing, the solemn act); the patriarch composing himself; the dignity of the deathbed scene (the blessing of faith, Hebrews 11:21). The verse displays Jacob rousing himself for Joseph's visit.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the rousing — "Israel strengthened himself, and sat upon the bed," the effort (Keil, Gill); "Israel strengthened himself," the resolve (Gill); "sat upon the bed," the dignity (Hebrews 11:21, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rousing Jacob. The verse marks the Joseph: coming. The verse displays the strength: gathered. The verse displays the bed: sat upon. The verse displays the blessing: prepared. The rousing displays Jacob composing himself for the act. The verse displays the Jacob: strengthening. The verse displays the bed: sat up. The verse displays the dignity: of the scene. Jacob rousing himself for Joseph's visit displays the resolve of the dying patriarch: Israel strengthened himself and sat upon the bed (Hebrews 11:21) — gathering his failing strength for the solemn blessing. The verse displays the composed Jacob, who recalls the promise (vv.3-4).
3:
"And Jacob said unto Joseph, God Almighty appeared unto me at Luz in the land of Canaan, and blessed me," The witnesses note Jacob recalling God's appearing at Luz. On "God Almighty appeared unto me at Luz in the land of Canaan," the grammatical voices note the appearing. Ellicott and Gill note that "God Almighty appeared unto me at Luz in the land of Canaan, and blessed me" — Jacob recalled God's appearing to him at Luz (Bethel, 28:19; 35:6-12) — the vision of the ladder and the covenant promise; the foundation of all that followed; the patriarch rehearsing the covenant before blessing the lads.

On "God Almighty," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the name. Calvin and Gill note "God Almighty" (El Shaddai, the name of covenant power and promise, 17:1; 35:11) — the God who had appeared and blessed; the almighty covenant God; the source of the blessing Jacob would now convey.

On "appeared unto me at Luz... and blessed me," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the covenant: "appeared unto me at Luz... and blessed me" — the appearing at Bethel (28:13-15; 35:9-12) where God blessed Jacob and renewed the covenant; the ground of Jacob's authority to bless and adopt (vv.4-5); the covenant rehearsed (to convey it to Joseph's sons). The verse displays Jacob recalling God's appearing at Luz.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the appearing — "God Almighty appeared unto me at Luz... and blessed me," the vision at Bethel (35:6-12, Ellicott, Gill); "God Almighty," El Shaddai (35:11, Calvin, Gill); "appeared unto me at Luz... and blessed me," the covenant (28:13, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the recalling Jacob. The verse marks the appearing: at Luz. The verse displays the God: Almighty. The verse displays the blessing: received. The verse displays the covenant: rehearsed. The recollection displays Jacob rehearsing the covenant. The verse displays the Jacob: recalling. The verse displays the Luz: Bethel. The verse displays the covenant: the ground. Jacob recalling God's appearing at Luz displays the covenant rehearsed: God Almighty appeared at Luz and blessed me (28:19; 35:6-12) — the vision and the covenant promise, the ground of the blessing Jacob would convey. The verse displays the recalling Jacob, rehearsing the promise (v.4).
4:
"And said unto me, Behold, I will make thee fruitful, and multiply thee, and I will make of thee a multitude of people; and will give this land to thy seed after thee for an everlasting possession." The witnesses note Jacob rehearsing the covenant promise. On "I will make thee fruitful, and multiply thee, and... a multitude of people," the grammatical voices note the promise. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "I will make thee fruitful, and multiply thee, and I will make of thee a multitude of people" — Jacob rehearsed the covenant promise (35:11): fruitfulness, multiplication, a multitude of people (the nation, indeed "a company of nations"); the promise of the seed; the covenant of the patriarchs (12:2; 17:4-6).

On "a multitude of people," the reformed JFB and witnesses note the nation. JFB and Gill note "a multitude of people" (a company of nations, 35:11) — the promise of the numerous seed (Israel, and indeed the tribes); the covenant multiplication; the ground of the adoption (Ephraim a multitude of nations, v.19).

On "will give this land to thy seed after thee for an everlasting possession," the grammatical-historical note the land: "will give this land to thy seed after thee for an everlasting possession" — the promise of Canaan (the everlasting possession, 17:8); the covenant land (to which God would bring them again, v.21); the ground of the inheritance Jacob conveyed (the lads sharing the covenant land, not the Egyptian); the providence (the covenant secured). The verse displays Jacob rehearsing the covenant promise.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "I will make thee fruitful... a multitude of people," the covenant (35:11, Keil, Gill); "a multitude of people," the nation (JFB, Gill); "give this land... for an everlasting possession," the land (17:8, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rehearsing Jacob. The verse marks the promise: fruitful, multiplied. The verse displays the people: a multitude. The verse displays the land: everlasting possession. The verse displays the covenant: rehearsed. The rehearsal displays Jacob conveying the covenant. The verse displays the Jacob: rehearsing. The verse displays the seed: multiplied. The verse displays the land: promised. Jacob rehearsing the covenant promise displays the ground of the blessing: I will multiply thee and give this land to thy seed for an everlasting possession (35:11; 17:8) — the covenant of fruitfulness and land, conveyed to Joseph's sons. The verse displays the rehearsing Jacob, who adopts the lads (v.5).
5:
"And now thy two sons, Ephraim and Manasseh, which were born unto thee in the land of Egypt before I came unto thee into Egypt, are mine; as Reuben and Simeon, they shall be mine." The witnesses note Jacob adopting Ephraim and Manasseh as his own — the crux of the adoption. On "thy two sons, Ephraim and Manasseh... are mine," interpreters expound the adoption. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note Jacob's act — "thy two sons, Ephraim and Manasseh... are mine; as Reuben and Simeon, they shall be mine": Jacob adopted Joseph's two sons as his own, reckoning them as his immediate sons (as Reuben and Simeon, his firstborn and second), each to be the head of a tribe in Israel; the adoption that gave Joseph the double portion of the firstborn (two tribes, Ephraim and Manasseh, where the other sons had but one each); the birthright (forfeited by Reuben, 49:3-4; 1 Chronicles 5:1-2) given to Joseph in this double portion; the elevation of Joseph's sons to the rank of patriarchs of tribes.

On "as Reuben and Simeon, they shall be mine," the wesleyan-arminian and witnesses note the rank. Gill notes that Ephraim and Manasseh were made equal to Reuben and Simeon (Jacob's own eldest sons) — each a tribe-head; the two sons of Joseph counted among the twelve (so that Joseph, through them, had the double portion); the birthright honor (the double portion, Deuteronomy 21:17) given to Joseph.

On the adoption, the puritan and grammatical-historical note the providence: Jacob's adoption (by the Spirit, foreseeing the tribes) — the double portion to Joseph (the beloved son of Rachel); the providence ordering the tribes (Ephraim and Manasseh among the twelve, Levi being set apart for the priesthood, so the number kept); the covenant blessing conveyed; the elevation of Joseph's seed. The verse displays Jacob adopting Ephraim and Manasseh as his own.

The witnesses agree the substance: Jacob adopted Joseph's two sons as his own, equal to Reuben and Simeon, each a tribe-head (Calvin, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the adoption: Ephraim and Manasseh made Jacob's immediate sons, each the head of a tribe — the double portion of the firstborn given to Joseph (1 Chronicles 5:1-2; Deuteronomy 21:17), the birthright (forfeited by Reuben) conveyed to the beloved son of Rachel; the providence ordering the tribes of Israel. The verse displays the adoption crux. The verse marks the sons: made his own. The verse displays the rank: as Reuben and Simeon. The verse displays the portion: double. The verse displays the birthright: to Joseph. The adoption displays Joseph's double portion in his sons. The verse displays the Jacob: adopting. The verse displays the sons: tribe-heads. The verse displays the providence: ordering the tribes. Jacob adopting Ephraim and Manasseh as his own displays the crux: they are mine, as Reuben and Simeon (1 Chronicles 5:1-2; Deuteronomy 21:17) — the double portion of the firstborn given to Joseph, his two sons made tribe-heads, the birthright conveyed. The verse displays the adopting Jacob, distinguishing later sons (v.6).
6:
"And thy issue, which thou begettest after them, shall be thine, and shall be called after the name of their brethren in their inheritance." The very brief verse notes Joseph's later sons reckoned under Ephraim and Manasseh. The grammatical voices note the distinction. Ellicott and Gill note that "thy issue, which thou begettest after them, shall be thine, and shall be called after the name of their brethren in their inheritance" — any later sons of Joseph (born after Ephraim and Manasseh) would be Joseph's own (not adopted as tribe-heads), but reckoned under the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh in their inheritance; the distinction (only the two firstborn adopted as tribes); the ordering of the inheritance.

On "shall be thine," the witnesses note the distinction. Gill notes that later sons would be Joseph's (not Jacob's adopted) — counted under Ephraim and Manasseh; the two firstborn alone the tribe-heads; the distinction preserved.

On "called after the name of their brethren in their inheritance," the grammatical-historical note the reckoning: "called after the name of their brethren in their inheritance" — later sons reckoned within the tribes of Ephraim and Manasseh (not separate tribes); the ordering of the inheritance; the providence keeping the number of the tribes; the double portion to Joseph in the two adopted sons. The verse displays Joseph's later sons reckoned under Ephraim and Manasseh.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the distinction — "thy issue... shall be thine, and shall be called after the name of their brethren," the later sons (Ellicott, Gill); "shall be thine," the distinction (Gill); "called after the name of their brethren in their inheritance," the reckoning (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the ordering Jacob. The verse marks the later sons: Joseph's. The verse displays the tribes: Ephraim and Manasseh. The verse displays the inheritance: reckoned. The verse displays the distinction: preserved. The distinction displays the ordering of Joseph's inheritance. The verse displays the Jacob: ordering. The verse displays the later sons: under the two. The verse displays the tribes: kept. Joseph's later sons reckoned under Ephraim and Manasseh displays the ordering: later issue called after their brethren in the inheritance (v.5) — only the two firstborn adopted as tribe-heads, the double portion to Joseph. The verse displays the ordering Jacob, who recalls Rachel (v.7).
7:
"And as for me, when I came from Padan, Rachel died by me in the land of Canaan in the way, when yet there was but a little way to come unto Ephrath: and I buried her there in the way of Ephrath; the same is Bethlehem." The witnesses note Jacob recalling Rachel's death and burial. On "when I came from Padan, Rachel died by me in the land of Canaan," the grammatical voices note the recollection. Ellicott and Gill note that "Rachel died by me in the land of Canaan in the way... to Ephrath: and I buried her there" — Jacob recalled the death of Rachel (Joseph's mother, 35:16-19) on the way to Ephrath (Bethlehem); the tender memory of the beloved wife; the mention (perhaps explaining why he honored her sons with the double portion, or why he buried her by the way, not in the cave of Machpelah); the recollection of Rachel.

On "Rachel died by me," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the love. Wesley and Calvin note Jacob's tender mention of Rachel (the beloved wife, 29:18, 30) — the grief still felt; the love for her that honored her son Joseph (and his sons, vv.5); the memory of the dear wife.

On "I buried her there in the way of Ephrath; the same is Bethlehem," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the place: "I buried her there in the way of Ephrath; the same is Bethlehem" — Rachel buried by the way (35:19-20), near Bethlehem (the place, the fathers note, near where the Messiah would be born, Micah 5:2; Matthew 2:1); the memory of the burial; the tender recollection (and, some note, an apology to Joseph for not burying his mother in Machpelah). The verse displays Jacob recalling Rachel's death and burial.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the recollection — "Rachel died by me in the land of Canaan... and I buried her there," Rachel's death (35:16-19, Ellicott, Gill); "Rachel died by me," the love (29:18, Wesley, Calvin); "I buried her there in the way of Ephrath; the same is Bethlehem," the place (35:19; Micah 5:2, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the recalling Jacob. The verse marks the Rachel: died. The verse displays the love: tender. The verse displays the burial: at Ephrath. The verse displays the Bethlehem: the place. The recollection displays Jacob's tender memory of Rachel. The verse displays the Jacob: recalling. The verse displays the Rachel: buried. The verse displays the Bethlehem: noted. Jacob recalling Rachel's death and burial displays the tender memory: Rachel died and was buried by the way to Ephrath, the same is Bethlehem (35:16-20; Micah 5:2) — the beloved wife remembered, her son Joseph honored with the double portion. The verse displays the recalling Jacob, who sees the lads (v.8).
8:
"And Israel beheld Joseph's sons, and said, Who are these?" The witnesses note Jacob's dim sight and his question about the lads. On "Israel beheld Joseph's sons," the grammatical voices note the sight. Henry and Gill note that "Israel beheld Joseph's sons, and said, Who are these?" — Jacob, his eyes dim with age (v.10), saw the lads but could not clearly discern them; the dimness of sight (as Isaac, 27:1); the question "Who are these?" (not knowing them by sight); the setting for the blessing (vv.9-20).

On "Who are these?" the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the question. Calvin and Gill note Jacob's question — asking who the lads were (his sight failing, v.10); the inquiry preparing for the blessing (Joseph's answer, v.9); the formal question (perhaps, the witnesses note, to draw out the solemn presentation, as in adoption).

On the question, the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the dimness: Jacob's dim sight (v.10) — the aged patriarch, like Isaac (27:1), not seeing clearly; the contrast (Isaac was deceived by his dim sight, 27:23; Jacob, though dim, blessed by faith and the Spirit, wittingly, v.14); the question that began the blessing. The verse displays Jacob's dim sight and his question.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sight — "Israel beheld Joseph's sons, and said, Who are these?" the dim sight (v.10, Henry, Gill); "Who are these?" the question (v.9, Calvin, Gill); the dimness (27:1, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dim-sighted Jacob. The verse marks the sight: dim. The verse displays the lads: beheld. The verse displays the question: who are these. The verse displays the blessing: beginning. The question displays Jacob's failing sight and the inquiry. The verse displays the Jacob: beholding. The verse displays the lads: unknown by sight. The verse displays the blessing: to come. Jacob's dim sight and his question about the lads display the setting of the blessing: Israel beheld the lads and asked, Who are these? (27:1; v.10) — the aged patriarch, dim of sight like Isaac, yet to bless by faith and the Spirit (v.14). The verse displays the inquiring Jacob, answered by Joseph (v.9).
9:
"And Joseph said unto his father, They are my sons, whom God hath given me in this place. And he said, Bring them, I pray thee, unto me, and I will bless them." The very brief verse notes Joseph presenting his sons for blessing. The grammatical voices note the answer. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Joseph said unto his father, They are my sons, whom God hath given me in this place" — Joseph answered that the lads were his sons, given him by God in Egypt; the godly acknowledgment (the sons God's gift, Psalm 127:3); the presentation for the blessing; Joseph owning God's gift.

On "whom God hath given me in this place," the witnesses note the gift. Gill notes Joseph's words — the sons "God hath given me" (the godly view of children, as God's gift, 33:5; Psalm 127:3); the acknowledgment of God; the gift owned even in Egypt.

On "Bring them, I pray thee, unto me, and I will bless them," the grammatical-historical note the blessing: "Bring them... and I will bless them" — Jacob's request to bring the lads near, that he might bless them (the patriarchal blessing, the conveying of the covenant); the solemn act prepared; the blessing of faith (Hebrews 11:21). The verse displays Joseph presenting his sons for blessing.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the answer — "They are my sons, whom God hath given me... Bring them... and I will bless them," the presentation (Psalm 127:3, Keil, Gill); "whom God hath given me in this place," the gift (33:5, Gill); "Bring them... and I will bless them," the blessing (Hebrews 11:21, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the presenting Joseph. The verse marks the sons: God's gift. The verse displays the place: Egypt. The verse displays the blessing: requested. The verse displays the act: prepared. The presentation displays Joseph offering his sons for the blessing. The verse displays the Joseph: presenting. The verse displays the sons: God's gift. The verse displays the blessing: to come. Joseph presenting his sons for blessing displays the godly acknowledgment: they are my sons, whom God hath given me; bring them, and I will bless them (33:5; Psalm 127:3; Hebrews 11:21) — the sons owned as God's gift, brought for the patriarchal blessing. The verse displays the presenting Joseph, the lads brought near (vv.10-13).
10:
"Now the eyes of Israel were dim for age, so that he could not see. And he brought them near unto him; and he kissed them, and embraced them." The very brief verse notes Jacob's dim eyes and his embrace of the lads. The grammatical voices note the dimness. Gill notes that "the eyes of Israel were dim for age, so that he could not see. And he brought them near... and he kissed them, and embraced them" — Jacob's eyes were dim with age (he could not see clearly, as Isaac, 27:1); the lads brought near; Jacob kissing and embracing them (the affection of the aged patriarch); the tender scene.

On "the eyes of Israel were dim for age," the witnesses note the age. Gill notes Jacob's dim sight (the infirmity of age, 27:1) — like Isaac before him; the dimness (yet, unlike Isaac, Jacob would bless wittingly, not deceived, v.14).

On "he kissed them, and embraced them," the grammatical-historical note the affection: "he kissed them, and embraced them" — Jacob's tender affection for the lads (kissing, embracing); the love of the grandfather; the tenderness preparing for the blessing (vv.11-20). The verse displays Jacob's dim eyes and his embrace of the lads.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the dimness — "the eyes of Israel were dim for age... and he kissed them, and embraced them," the dim sight and embrace (27:1, Gill); "the eyes of Israel were dim for age," the age (Gill); "he kissed them, and embraced them," the affection (vv.11-20, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the embracing Jacob. The verse marks the eyes: dim. The verse displays the lads: brought near. The verse displays the embrace: tender. The verse displays the blessing: to come. The embrace displays Jacob's affection for the lads. The verse displays the Jacob: embracing. The verse displays the lads: kissed. The verse displays the love: of the grandfather. Jacob's dim eyes and his embrace of the lads display the tender scene: his eyes dim, he kissed and embraced them (27:1) — the aged patriarch, dim of sight, embracing his grandsons before the blessing. The verse displays the embracing Jacob, marveling at the mercy (v.11).
11:
"And Israel said unto Joseph, I had not thought to see thy face: and, lo, God hath shewed me also thy seed." The witnesses note Jacob marveling at God's mercy beyond his hope. On "I had not thought to see thy face," the grammatical voices note the wonder. Wesley and Gill note that "Israel said unto Joseph, I had not thought to see thy face" — Jacob marveled: he had never thought to see Joseph's face again (having mourned him as dead, 37:34-35; 42:36); the wonder of the reunion (beyond all his hope); the mercy of God past expectation.

On "lo, God hath shewed me also thy seed," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the abundance. Wesley and Gill note that God had shown Jacob not only Joseph (whom he had not thought to see) but "also thy seed" (his sons) — the mercy beyond expectation (not only the son restored, but his grandsons too); the abundant goodness of God; the overflowing mercy.

On the wonder, the reformed and grammatical-historical note the providence: Jacob's wonder at God's mercy — the goodness of God, who gives beyond our asking or thinking (Ephesians 3:20); the providence crowning the long sorrow with abundant joy (Joseph alive, exalted, and his seed before Jacob); the mercy past hope. The verse displays Jacob marveling at God's mercy beyond his hope.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the wonder — "I had not thought to see thy face: and, lo, God hath shewed me also thy seed," the mercy beyond hope (37:34, Wesley, Gill); "I had not thought to see thy face," the wonder (42:36, Gill); "God hath shewed me also thy seed," the abundance (Ephesians 3:20, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the marveling Jacob. The verse marks the face: not thought to see. The verse displays the seed: shown also. The verse displays the mercy: beyond hope. The verse displays the providence: abundant. The wonder displays Jacob's joy at God's mercy. The verse displays the Jacob: marveling. The verse displays the Joseph: seen. The verse displays the seed: shown. Jacob marveling at God's mercy beyond his hope displays the abundant goodness of God: I had not thought to see thy face, and God hath shewed me also thy seed (37:34; Ephesians 3:20) — the mercy past expectation, not only the son restored but his seed before Jacob. The verse displays the marveling Jacob, who sets the lads for blessing (vv.12-13).
12:
"And Joseph brought them out from between his knees, and he bowed himself with his face to the earth." The very brief verse notes Joseph presenting the lads with reverence. The grammatical voices note the act. Ellicott and Gill note that "Joseph brought them out from between his knees, and he bowed himself with his face to the earth" — Joseph took the lads from between Jacob's knees (where they had been, near the patriarch) and bowed himself to the earth (in reverence before his father, and for the blessing); the reverence of the great ruler before the aged patriarch; the humble homage (a further fulfillment of the dreams, 37:7-10, Joseph and his bowing before the father).

On "brought them out from between his knees," the witnesses note the presentation. Gill notes Joseph taking the lads from Jacob's knees (the place of nearness, or of adoption) — to present them rightly for the blessing (vv.13-14); the arranging of the lads.

On "he bowed himself with his face to the earth," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the reverence: "he bowed himself with his face to the earth" — Joseph's deep reverence before his father (the great ruler of Egypt humbling himself before the dying patriarch); the honor to the father (Exodus 20:12); the homage for the blessing; the reverence of the godly son. The verse displays Joseph presenting the lads with reverence.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the act — "Joseph brought them out from between his knees, and he bowed himself with his face to the earth," the reverence (37:7, Ellicott, Gill); "brought them out from between his knees," the presentation (vv.13-14, Gill); "he bowed himself with his face to the earth," the reverence (Exodus 20:12, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the reverent Joseph. The verse marks the lads: presented. The verse displays the bow: to the earth. The verse displays the reverence: deep. The verse displays the honor: to the father. The act displays Joseph's reverence before his father. The verse displays the Joseph: bowing. The verse displays the lads: arranged. The verse displays the honor: shown. Joseph presenting the lads with reverence displays his honor to the father: Joseph bowed himself to the earth (37:7; Exodus 20:12) — the great ruler humbling himself before the dying patriarch, presenting the lads for the blessing. The verse displays the arranging Joseph, the lads set (vv.13-14).
13:
"And Joseph took them both, Ephraim in his right hand toward Israel's left hand, and Manasseh in his left hand toward Israel's right hand, and brought them near unto him." The very brief verse notes Joseph arranging the lads for the blessing. The grammatical voices note the arrangement. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Joseph took them both, Ephraim in his right hand toward Israel's left hand, and Manasseh in his left hand toward Israel's right hand" — Joseph carefully placed the lads: Ephraim (the younger) at Jacob's left, Manasseh (the elder) at Jacob's right; the arrangement intended that Jacob's right hand (the hand of the chief blessing) should fall on Manasseh the firstborn; the careful ordering by the right of primogeniture.

On "Ephraim in his right hand toward Israel's left hand," the witnesses note the order. Gill notes Joseph's arrangement — Ephraim (younger) to Jacob's left, Manasseh (elder) to Jacob's right; the intent that the elder receive the right-hand blessing; the natural order.

On "brought them near unto him," the grammatical-historical note the design: "brought them near unto him" — Joseph bringing the lads so arranged (for the blessing); the design that Manasseh (the firstborn) receive the right hand; the order that Jacob would cross (v.14), guiding his hands wittingly to prefer Ephraim. The verse displays Joseph arranging the lads for the blessing.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the arrangement — "Ephraim in his right hand toward Israel's left hand, and Manasseh in his left hand toward Israel's right hand," the careful placing (Keil, Gill); "Ephraim... toward Israel's left hand," the order (Gill); "brought them near unto him," the design (v.14, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the arranging Joseph. The verse marks the Ephraim: at the left. The verse displays the Manasseh: at the right. The verse displays the design: the elder to the right. The verse displays the crossing: to come. The arrangement displays Joseph ordering by primogeniture. The verse displays the Joseph: arranging. The verse displays the lads: placed. The verse displays the right hand: for the elder. Joseph arranging the lads for the blessing displays the design by primogeniture: Ephraim to Jacob's left, Manasseh to his right (v.14) — that the elder receive the right-hand blessing, the order Jacob would cross. The verse displays the arranged lads, and Jacob's crossed hands (v.14).
14:
"And Israel stretched out his right hand, and laid it upon Ephraim's head, who was the younger, and his left hand upon Manasseh's head, guiding his hands wittingly; for Manasseh was the firstborn." The witnesses note Jacob crossing his hands to prefer Ephraim — the crux of the sovereign election. On "Israel stretched out his right hand, and laid it upon Ephraim's head, who was the younger," the grammatical voices note the crossing. Ellicott and Gill note that "Israel stretched out his right hand, and laid it upon Ephraim's head, who was the younger, and his left hand upon Manasseh's head" — Jacob crossed his arms (or hands), laying his right hand (the hand of the chief blessing) on Ephraim the younger, and his left on Manasseh the elder (reversing Joseph's arrangement, v.13); the crossing of the hands; the preferring of the younger.

On "guiding his hands wittingly," interpreters expound the deliberate act. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note "guiding his hands wittingly" — Jacob did this knowingly, deliberately (not by mistake, as his dim eyes might have caused, v.10, but "wittingly," well knowing what he did); the crossing was no error but a deliberate, Spirit-led act; Jacob, blessing by faith (Hebrews 11:21) and prophetic foresight, deliberately preferred Ephraim (the younger) over Manasseh (the elder); the sovereign election of the younger (as God had chosen Jacob over Esau, 25:23; Isaac over Ishmael; Abel over Cain; the grace that often passes by the elder for the younger, contrary to nature's order, that the election might be of grace, not of birth, Romans 9:11-13); the prophetic preferring (foreseeing Ephraim's greater future, v.19).

On "for Manasseh was the firstborn," the wesleyan-arminian and grammatical-historical note the reversal: "for Manasseh was the firstborn" — the note that Manasseh was the elder (yet the younger preferred); the reversal of the natural order; the sovereign grace (the pattern of election, the younger preferred); the act of faith (Hebrews 11:21, "by faith Jacob blessed both the sons of Joseph"). The verse displays Jacob crossing his hands to prefer Ephraim.

The witnesses agree the substance: Jacob deliberately crossed his hands, the right on Ephraim the younger, guiding them wittingly (Ellicott, Calvin, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the sovereign election: the crossing was no error of dim sight but a deliberate, Spirit-led, prophetic act ("wittingly") — Jacob, by faith (Hebrews 11:21), preferring the younger over the elder, as God had chosen Jacob over Esau (25:23), Isaac over Ishmael, the grace that passes by the firstborn that election might be of grace, not birth (Romans 9:11-13); foreseeing Ephraim's greater future (v.19). The verse displays the election crux. The verse marks the right hand: on Ephraim. The verse displays the wittingly: deliberate. The verse displays the younger: preferred. The verse displays the election: sovereign. The crossing displays Jacob's prophetic, sovereign preferring of the younger. The verse displays the Jacob: crossing. The verse displays the Ephraim: preferred. The verse displays the grace: electing. Jacob crossing his hands to prefer Ephraim displays the crux: he guided his hands wittingly, the right on Ephraim the younger (25:23; Romans 9:11-13; Hebrews 11:21) — the deliberate, Spirit-led preferring of the younger, the sovereign election of grace over nature's order. The verse displays the blessing Jacob (vv.15-16).
15:
"And he blessed Joseph, and said, God, before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac did walk, the God which fed me all my life long unto this day," The witnesses note Jacob blessing by the covenant God who shepherded him. On "God, before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac did walk," the grammatical voices note the covenant God. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he blessed Joseph, and said, God, before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac did walk" — Jacob blessed (Joseph, in his sons) invoking the covenant God, before whom Abraham and Isaac had walked (in faith and obedience, 17:1; 24:40); the God of the fathers; the covenant God invoked in the blessing.

On "the God which fed me all my life long unto this day," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the shepherd-God. Wesley and Calvin note the tender phrase — "the God which fed me (Hebrew, shepherded me) all my life long unto this day": Jacob owned God as his shepherd, who had fed and led him all his life (the Shepherd of Israel, Psalm 23:1; 80:1; the God who had kept him through all his wanderings and troubles); the grateful testimony of the aged saint to God's lifelong care; the first calling of God a Shepherd (a name dear to the church, John 10:11).

On the blessing, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the faith: Jacob's blessing (invoking the covenant, shepherding God) — the faith that owned God's lifelong faithfulness; the blessing of the dying patriarch (by faith, Hebrews 11:21); the covenant conveyed through the God of the fathers and the Shepherd of his life. The verse displays Jacob blessing by the covenant God who shepherded him.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the covenant God — "God, before whom my fathers Abraham and Isaac did walk," the God of the fathers (17:1, Keil, Gill); "the God which fed me all my life long," the Shepherd-God (Psalm 23:1, Wesley, Calvin); the faith (Hebrews 11:21, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessing Jacob. The verse marks the God: of the fathers. The verse displays the shepherd: who fed me. The verse displays the life: all long. The verse displays the faith: owning it. The blessing displays Jacob invoking the covenant Shepherd-God. The verse displays the Jacob: blessing. The verse displays the God: the Shepherd. The verse displays the faithfulness: lifelong. Jacob blessing by the covenant God who shepherded him displays the grateful faith: the God of Abraham and Isaac, who fed me all my life long (17:1; Psalm 23:1) — the covenant God and the Shepherd of his life invoked in the blessing. The verse displays the blessing Jacob, invoking the Angel-Redeemer (v.16).
16:
"The Angel which redeemed me from all evil, bless the lads; and let my name be named on them, and the name of my fathers Abraham and Isaac; and let them grow into a multitude in the midst of the earth." The witnesses note the Angel-Redeemer invoked in the blessing — the crux of the redeeming Angel. On "The Angel which redeemed me from all evil," interpreters expound the Angel. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the great phrase — "The Angel which redeemed me from all evil, bless the lads": Jacob invoked "the Angel" who had redeemed him from all evil; the witnesses identify this Angel — not a created angel, but the Angel of the LORD, the Angel of God's presence (Exodus 23:20-21; Isaiah 63:9), the same with the God invoked in v.15 (the blessing being one, addressed to God and the Angel as one); the divine Angel, the Angel of the covenant (Malachi 3:1), whom many of the fathers and the Reformed identify as the pre-incarnate Christ (the second Person, the Angel-Jehovah who appeared to the patriarchs, 16:7-13; 22:11-18; 31:11-13; 32:24-30); the proof: Jacob asks this Angel to "bless the lads" (a divine act, prayer addressed to him as God); the "Angel" and "God" (v.15) joined as one in the blessing.

On "which redeemed me from all evil," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the redemption. Wesley and Gill note "redeemed" (Hebrew goel, the kinsman-redeemer) — the first use of "redeem" in Scripture; the Angel who had redeemed (delivered, rescued) Jacob from all evil (from Esau, Laban, all his troubles); the Redeemer (a name pointing to Christ, the Goel, the Kinsman-Redeemer, Job 19:25; the great Redeemer from all evil, sin, and death); the testimony of the aged saint to his Redeemer.

On "bless the lads; and let my name be named on them... and let them grow into a multitude," the grammatical-historical note the blessing: "bless the lads; and let my name be named on them, and the name of my fathers... and let them grow into a multitude" — the blessing proper: that the Angel bless the lads, that Jacob's name (and Abraham's and Isaac's) be named on them (they reckoned among the covenant seed, the heirs of the promise), and that they grow into a multitude (the great increase, fulfilled, Numbers 1; Ephraim and Manasseh great tribes); the covenant blessing conveyed; the increase promised. The verse displays the Angel-Redeemer invoked in the blessing.

The witnesses agree the substance: Jacob invoked the Angel who had redeemed him to bless the lads, name them in the covenant, and make them a multitude (Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The phrase bears its weight as the crux of the redeeming Angel: "the Angel which redeemed me from all evil" — not a created angel, but the Angel of the LORD, the Angel of the covenant, joined as one with the God of v.15 (asked to "bless," a divine act), whom many identify as the pre-incarnate Christ (Exodus 23:20-21; Malachi 3:1); "redeemed" (goel) the first use of the word, the Redeemer pointing to Christ the Kinsman-Redeemer (Job 19:25). The verse displays the Angel-Redeemer crux. The verse marks the Angel: the Redeemer. The verse displays the Christ: foreshadowed. The Angel displays the divine Redeemer invoked in the blessing. The Angel-Redeemer invoked in the blessing displays the crux: the Angel which redeemed me from all evil, bless the lads (Exodus 23:20-21; Malachi 3:1; Job 19:25) — the divine Angel of the covenant, joined as one with God (asked to bless), the Redeemer (goel) pointing to Christ, invoked to bless and multiply the lads. The verse displays the blessing Jacob, displeasing Joseph (vv.17-18).
17:
"And when Joseph saw that his father laid his right hand upon the head of Ephraim, it displeased him: and he held up his father's hand, to remove it from Ephraim's head unto Manasseh's head." The witnesses note Joseph displeased and trying to redirect his father's hand. On "when Joseph saw that his father laid his right hand upon the head of Ephraim, it displeased him," the grammatical voices note the displeasure. Gill and Calvin note that "when Joseph saw that his father laid his right hand upon the head of Ephraim, it displeased him" — Joseph, seeing the right hand (the chief blessing) on Ephraim the younger (not Manasseh the elder, as he had arranged, v.13), was displeased; the natural concern (that the firstborn should have the chief blessing); the supposing it an error of Jacob's dim sight (v.10).

On "it displeased him," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the misunderstanding. Calvin and Gill note Joseph's displeasure — supposing Jacob mistaken (by his dim eyes); the natural regard for the firstborn's right; the misunderstanding (not yet knowing Jacob acted wittingly and prophetically, v.14).

On "he held up his father's hand, to remove it from Ephraim's head unto Manasseh's head," the grammatical-historical note the attempt: "he held up his father's hand, to remove it" — Joseph tried to redirect Jacob's right hand to Manasseh; the attempt to correct the supposed error; the natural effort (to secure the firstborn's blessing); the attempt Jacob would refuse (vv.18-19). The verse displays Joseph displeased and trying to redirect his father's hand.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the displeasure — "it displeased him: and he held up his father's hand, to remove it," Joseph's concern (v.13, Gill, Calvin); "it displeased him," the misunderstanding (v.14, Calvin, Gill); "he held up his father's hand, to remove it," the attempt (vv.18-19, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the concerned Joseph. The verse marks the right hand: on Ephraim. The verse displays the displeasure: Joseph's. The verse displays the attempt: to redirect. The verse displays the firstborn: Manasseh. The displeasure displays Joseph's regard for the firstborn. The verse displays the Joseph: displeased. The verse displays the hand: held up. The verse displays the error: supposed. Joseph displeased and trying to redirect his father's hand displays the misunderstanding: Joseph held up his father's hand to move it to Manasseh (v.13) — supposing an error of dim sight, not knowing Jacob acted wittingly (v.14). The verse displays the correcting Joseph, refused by Jacob (vv.18-19).
18:
"And Joseph said unto his father, Not so, my father: for this is the firstborn; put thy right hand upon his head." The very brief verse notes Joseph urging the right hand on the firstborn. The grammatical voices note the plea. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Joseph said unto his father, Not so, my father: for this is the firstborn; put thy right hand upon his head" — Joseph urged his father: "Not so" — Manasseh was the firstborn, and should have the right hand (the chief blessing); the plea for the firstborn's right; the natural insistence (supposing Jacob mistaken, v.17); the correction urged.

On "for this is the firstborn," the witnesses note the right. Gill notes Joseph's appeal — Manasseh the firstborn (the right of primogeniture); the natural claim; the plea that the elder receive the chief blessing.

On "put thy right hand upon his head," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the urging: "put thy right hand upon his head" — Joseph urging the right hand to Manasseh; the insistence on the firstborn's blessing; the correction Jacob would refuse (v.19, "I know it, my son"); the natural order pressed. The verse displays Joseph urging the right hand on the firstborn.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the plea — "Not so, my father: for this is the firstborn; put thy right hand upon his head," the urging (v.17, Keil, Gill); "for this is the firstborn," the right (Gill); "put thy right hand upon his head," the urging (v.19, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the urging Joseph. The verse marks the plea: not so. The verse displays the firstborn: Manasseh. The verse displays the right hand: urged. The verse displays the correction: pressed. The plea displays Joseph urging the firstborn's right. The verse displays the Joseph: urging. The verse displays the Manasseh: the firstborn. The verse displays the refusal: to come. Joseph urging the right hand on the firstborn displays the natural plea: not so; this is the firstborn; put thy right hand on his head (v.17) — the insistence on Manasseh's right, the correction Jacob would refuse (v.19). The verse displays the refused Joseph, answered by Jacob (v.19).
19:
"And his father refused, and said, I know it, my son, I know it: he also shall become a people, and he also shall be great: but truly his younger brother shall be greater than he, and his seed shall become a multitude of nations." The witnesses note Jacob foretelling Ephraim's greater future — the crux of the younger preferred. On "his father refused, and said, I know it, my son, I know it," the grammatical voices note the deliberate act. Ellicott and Gill note that "his father refused, and said, I know it, my son, I know it" — Jacob refused to move his hand, affirming twice "I know it" — he knew Manasseh was the firstborn (v.14), and acted not by error but by deliberate, prophetic intent; the confirmation that the crossing was wittingly done (v.14); the firm refusal.

On "his younger brother shall be greater than he, and his seed shall become a multitude of nations," interpreters expound the prophecy. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note Jacob's prophecy — "he also shall become a people... but truly his younger brother shall be greater... his seed shall become a multitude of nations": Jacob foretold that both would become peoples (Manasseh great too), but that Ephraim (the younger) would be the greater, his seed a "multitude of nations" (literally, "fulness of the nations"); the prophecy fulfilled — Ephraim became the larger and leading tribe (the chief of the ten northern tribes, often called by Ephraim's name, Hosea, Isaiah; Ephraim more numerous, Numbers 1:33, 35, though by Manasseh's later growth the matter varied); the sovereign election of the younger (the grace that prefers the lesser, as Jacob over Esau, 25:23; the prophetic foresight); the divine ordering of the tribes.

On the prophecy, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the providence: Jacob's foretelling (by the Spirit) Ephraim's greatness — the prophecy of the younger's preeminence; the providence ordering the tribes; the election of grace (the younger over the elder, contrary to nature, that the choice might be God's). The verse displays Jacob foretelling Ephraim's greater future.

The witnesses agree the substance: Jacob refused, knowing what he did, and foretold that the younger Ephraim would be greater, his seed a multitude of nations (Ellicott, Calvin, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the younger preferred: Jacob's deliberate, prophetic act ("I know it") confirmed — both would be peoples, but Ephraim the younger the greater (fulfilled in Ephraim's leading the northern tribes); the sovereign election of the younger over the elder (as Jacob over Esau, 25:23), the grace that prefers the lesser, the prophetic foresight ordering the tribes. The verse displays the younger-preferred crux. The verse marks the refusal: I know it. The verse displays the prophecy: the younger greater. The verse displays the multitude: of nations. The verse displays the election: of grace. The prophecy displays Jacob's deliberate preferring of the younger. The verse displays the Jacob: foretelling. The verse displays the Ephraim: the greater. The verse displays the election: sovereign. Jacob foretelling Ephraim's greater future displays the crux: I know it; the younger shall be greater, his seed a multitude of nations (25:23) — the deliberate, prophetic preferring of the younger, the sovereign election of grace fulfilled in Ephraim's preeminence. The verse displays the blessing Jacob, setting Ephraim first (v.20).
20:
"And he blessed them that day, saying, In thee shall Israel bless, saying, God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh: and he set Ephraim before Manasseh." The witnesses note Jacob making the lads a pattern of blessing, Ephraim first. On "In thee shall Israel bless, saying, God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh," the grammatical voices note the pattern. Ellicott and Gill note that "he blessed them... In thee shall Israel bless, saying, God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh" — Jacob made Ephraim and Manasseh a proverb of blessing in Israel: that the people would bless their children with "God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh" (a form of blessing, naming the two as the pattern of the blessed); the honor put on the lads; the form of blessing in Israel (used to this day among the Jews).

On "God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh," the reformed and witnesses note the honor. Gill notes the form of blessing (Ephraim first, the preferred) — the two made the pattern of the blessed in Israel; the high honor; the proverb of blessing.

On "he set Ephraim before Manasseh," the grammatical-historical note the order: "he set Ephraim before Manasseh" — Jacob naming Ephraim first (the younger before the elder); the confirmation of the preferring (vv.14, 19); the order of the tribes set (Ephraim first); the sovereign election sealed in the form of blessing. The verse displays Jacob making the lads a pattern of blessing, Ephraim first.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the pattern — "In thee shall Israel bless... God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh," the proverb of blessing (Ellicott, Gill); "God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh," the honor (Gill); "he set Ephraim before Manasseh," the order (vv.14, 19, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessing Jacob. The verse marks the pattern: of blessing. The verse displays the form: as Ephraim and Manasseh. The verse displays the Ephraim: set first. The verse displays the election: sealed. The pattern displays the lads made the proverb of blessing. The verse displays the Jacob: blessing. The verse displays the lads: the pattern. The verse displays the Ephraim: preferred. Jacob making the lads a pattern of blessing, Ephraim first, displays the honor and the election sealed: God make thee as Ephraim and as Manasseh, Ephraim set first (vv.14, 19) — the two made the proverb of blessing in Israel, the younger preferred. The verse displays the blessing Jacob, promising the return (v.21).
21:
"And Israel said unto Joseph, Behold, I die: but God shall be with you, and bring you again unto the land of your fathers." The witnesses note Jacob's dying assurance of the return to Canaan. On "Behold, I die," the grammatical voices note the death. Gill and Calvin note that "Israel said unto Joseph, Behold, I die" — Jacob announced his approaching death (the dying patriarch); the solemn farewell; the faith that faced death calmly (his hope in the covenant promise); the dying testimony.

On "God shall be with you," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the presence. Wesley and Gill note Jacob's assurance — "God shall be with you" (the covenant God's presence with Israel in Egypt, as promised, 46:4); the comfort to Joseph and his seed; the faith that God would not forsake them.

On "bring you again unto the land of your fathers," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the return: "bring you again unto the land of your fathers" — Jacob's dying faith in the return to Canaan (the Exodus, foretold, 15:13-16; 46:4); the assurance that Egypt was not their home, but Canaan (the covenant land); the faith that looked beyond the present sojourn to the promised inheritance (Hebrews 11:13-16, 22; the dying patriarch's hope, echoed in Joseph's own dying charge, 50:24-25). The verse displays Jacob's dying assurance of the return to Canaan.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the death — "Behold, I die: but God shall be with you, and bring you again," the dying faith (Gill, Calvin); "God shall be with you," the presence (46:4, Wesley, Gill); "bring you again unto the land of your fathers," the return (15:13-16; 50:24, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dying Jacob. The verse marks the death: I die. The verse displays the presence: God with you. The verse displays the return: to Canaan. The verse displays the faith: in the promise. The assurance displays Jacob's dying hope in the covenant. The verse displays the Jacob: dying. The verse displays the God: with them. The verse displays the return: promised. Jacob's dying assurance of the return to Canaan displays the covenant faith: I die, but God shall be with you and bring you again to the land of your fathers (46:4; 15:13-16; 50:24) — the dying patriarch's hope, that Egypt was not their home but Canaan the promised inheritance. The verse displays the dying Jacob, giving Joseph the portion (v.22).
22:
"Moreover I have given to thee one portion above thy brethren, which I took out of the hand of the Amorite with my sword and with my bow." The witnesses note Jacob giving Joseph the portion of Shechem. On "I have given to thee one portion above thy brethren," the grammatical voices note the gift. Ellicott and Gill note that "I have given to thee one portion above thy brethren" — Jacob gave Joseph one portion (Hebrew shechem, a play on the place Shechem) above his brethren; the special gift (the double portion of the firstborn, in his sons, vv.5; and this portion of land); the honor to Joseph (the beloved son); the portion above the brethren.

On "one portion," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the Shechem. Calvin and Gill note "one portion" (shechem) — likely the parcel of ground at Shechem (33:19, bought by Jacob; where Joseph would be buried, Joshua 24:32); the special inheritance; the gift to Joseph (the word a play on Shechem).

On "which I took out of the hand of the Amorite with my sword and with my bow," interpreters note the conquest. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the phrase — "which I took out of the hand of the Amorite with my sword and with my bow": variously understood — (a) the parcel at Shechem (bought, 33:19, but perhaps later seized or recovered from the Amorites, or defended by the sword after the slaughter, ch. 34); (b) a prophetic gift (Jacob, by faith, giving in prospect the land the seed would take from the Amorites by sword and bow, the conquest, Joshua); (c) "sword and bow" as Jacob's prayer and faith (the Targum: "my prayer and supplication"); the witnesses agree the portion was Joseph's special inheritance (the Shechem parcel, where his bones were laid, Joshua 24:32), given by the dying patriarch. The verse displays Jacob giving Joseph the portion of Shechem.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the gift — "I have given to thee one portion above thy brethren," the special portion (vv.5, Ellicott, Gill); "one portion," the Shechem parcel (33:19; Joshua 24:32, Calvin, Gill); "which I took out of the hand of the Amorite with my sword and with my bow," the conquest (ch. 34, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the giving Jacob. The verse marks the portion: above the brethren. The verse displays the Shechem: the parcel. The verse displays the conquest: sword and bow. The verse displays the inheritance: Joseph's. The gift displays Joseph's special portion. The verse displays the Jacob: giving. The verse displays the Joseph: honored. The verse displays the Shechem: his portion. Jacob giving Joseph the portion of Shechem displays the special inheritance: one portion above thy brethren, taken from the Amorite (33:19; Joshua 24:32) — the Shechem parcel, where Joseph's bones would be laid, given to the beloved son. The verse closes the chapter, before Jacob's blessing of all the sons (ch.49).
